perturbation.original_text: The federal and state courts within the State of New York shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The courts within the State of New York may have jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall have exclusive jurisdiction' with 'may have jurisdiction' introduces ambiguity regarding where disputes can be resolved, potentially conflicting with legal precedents ensuring clarity in jurisdictional clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 301
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 301 establishes the basis for personal jurisdiction over persons or property within New York. By making jurisdiction 'may have' instead of 'shall have exclusive', it goes against established business rules that are clearly defined within NY's jurisdiction.

Jurisdiction over persons, property or status. A court may exercise such jurisdiction over persons, property, or status as might have been exercised heretofore.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates exclusive jurisdiction in New York courts, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the courts 'may have jurisdiction'. This weakens the legal obligation for disputes to be resolved exclusively in New York, potentially allowing for litigation elsewhere. The original text provides certainty, whereas the changed text creates uncertainty regarding jurisdiction. While the provided snippet from NY CPLR § 301 doesn't directly address exclusive jurisdiction clauses, the change introduces ambiguity around established jurisdiction, potentially conflicting with clear business rules within New York as noted in the LLM's law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned without consent of Submitting Party .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be assigned with a reasonable attempt to gain consent of Submitting Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may not be assigned without consent' to 'may be assigned with a reasonable attempt to gain consent' introduces ambiguity around the assignment clause. This weakens the Submitting Party's control over who the agreement can be assigned to.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 13-101 pertains to the transfer of claims and rights. The law aims for clarity in contractual assignments. Altering this section to include 'reasonable attempt' could lead to disputes over what constitutes a reasonable attempt and circumvents the need for explicit consent, leading to possible legal action.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 13 Transfer of Obligations and Rights TITLE 1 Transferability; Effect of Transfer up TITLE 1 Transferability; Effect of Transfer next SECTION 13-103 Transfer of judgment for sum of money This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 13-101 Transfer of claims General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 13, TITLE 1 § 13-101. Transfer of claims. Any claim or demand can be transferred, except in one of the following cases: 1. Where it is to recover damages for a personal injury; 2. Where it is founded upon a grant, which is made void by a statute of the state; or upon a claim to or interest in real property, a grant of which, by the transferrer, would be void by such a statute; 3. Where a transfer thereof is expressly forbidden by: (a) a statute of the state, or (b) a statute of the United States, or (c) would contravene public policy.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'may not be assigned without consent' to 'may be assigned with a reasonable attempt to gain consent' introduces ambiguity and weakens the requirement for explicit consent, potentially leading to disputes. NY General Obligations Law § 13-101 aims for clarity in contractual assignments. While the snippet doesn't directly forbid the 'reasonable attempt' language, it emphasizes the transferability of claims unless expressly forbidden by statute or contravening public policy. The altered text undermines the clear consent requirement, introducing a subjective standard of 'reasonable attempt,' which could contravene the law's intent for clear and enforceable agreements on claim transfers, thus creating a contradiction by introducing ambiguity to the assignment clause.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees it will not in any form export, reexport, resell, ship or divert or cause to be exported, reexported, resold, shipped or diverted, directly or indirectly, any product or technical data or software furnished hereunder or the direct product of such technical data or software to any country for which the United States Government or any agency thereof at the time of export and reexport requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient should endeavor to not in any form export, reexport, resell, ship or divert or cause to be exported, reexported, resold, shipped or diverted, directly or indirectly, any product or technical data or software furnished hereunder or the direct product of such technical data or software to any country.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees it will not' with 'should endeavor to not' weakens the obligation and makes it non-committal. The phrase 'for which the United States Government or any agency thereof at the time of export and reexport requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval' has been removed, which further muddies the waters and makes the recipient seem to be not required to obtain any export licenses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR), specifically 15 CFR Parts 730-774, mandate strict compliance with export control laws and require obtaining necessary licenses for exporting controlled items. Changing the language to 'should endeavor to not' and removing the clause about obtaining licenses undermines the mandatory nature of these regulations, potentially leading to illegal export activities and violations of US law.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees it will not' to 'should endeavor to not' weakens the obligation concerning export regulations. The original text clearly prohibits exporting without proper licensing. The changed text uses suggestive language, diluting the legally binding nature of export compliance. Additionally, the removal of 'for which the United States Government or any agency thereof at the time of export and reexport requires an export license or other governmental approval without first obtaining such license or approval' directly contradicts export control regulations that mandate obtaining licenses. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and refers to 15 CFR Chapter VII Subchapter C, indicating that the Department of Commerce has export regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2 highlights the Commerce Control List (CCL) under the EAR, emphasizing the necessity to adhere to export regulations which further underscores the contradiction created by weakening the original obligation.
